question: Strait of Hormuz traffic returns to normal by end of June?

key_actors: IMF PortWatch as the resolving data source
Commercial shipping operators and charterers deciding whether to transit the Strait of Hormuz
Marine insurers and war-risk underwriters affecting voyage viability
Iran and the IRGC Navy, which can threaten or disrupt transit
United States and partner navies providing deterrence and security
Gulf oil and trade exporters whose cargo demand drives vessel flows
Maritime data providers and AIS infrastructure affecting observed traffic counts
key_mechanisms: Regional conflict and maritime threats alter shipowner willingness to transit
War-risk insurance pricing and coverage constraints suppress or permit traffic recovery
Actual vessel transits determine IMF PortWatch daily arrivals and the published 7-day moving average
The 7-day moving-average rule creates mechanical lag, so even a rebound may not reach 60 quickly
A single published qualifying date at or above 60 is sufficient for YES, including in-window revisions
AIS disruption, spoofing, or vessels going dark can affect measured traffic in the PortWatch series
latent_variables: The latest unpublished or recently updated IMF PortWatch 7-day moving average level
The speed of any traffic rebound during the remainder of June 2026
Probability of additional military or harassment incidents before June 30, 2026
Insurer willingness to keep offering cover at tolerable prices
Shipowner and charterer risk tolerance after months of disruption
How much AIS impairment is depressing reported counts relative to actual traffic

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: IMF PortWatch publishes a 7-day moving average for Strait of Hormuz 'Arrivals of Ships' equal to or above 60 on at least one date between market creation and June 30, 2026.
supporting_evidence: The market only requires one qualifying published date at or above 60, not sustained normalization, which leaves a narrow path to YES via a sudden rebound or in-window revision. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/faqs

PortWatch notes AIS limitations during the conflict, including GPS jamming, AIS spoofing, and vessels going dark, which creates some uncertainty around measured traffic. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/chokepoint6

PortWatch methodology and weekly updates imply that revisions and new Tuesday publications can still alter the observed series before final resolution. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/data-and-methodology

New evidence indicates war-risk premiums for Gulf cargoes transiting Hormuz fell sharply to 0.35% from 1.7% a week earlier, while tanker loadings booked for June 8-14 rose to 41 from 18 in the prior seven-day window, suggesting owners are returning and traffic could rebound faster than previously expected.
contradicting_evidence: A PortWatch-linked series showed the Strait of Hormuz 7-day moving average at about 6.00 on May 24, 2026, far below the 60 threshold required for YES. https://en.macromicro.me/series/30932/imf-hormuz-number-total-ma7
PortWatch lists the Hormuz disruption as an ongoing event with reduced traffic since February 28, 2026. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/cc317ba850e34c4dadbead6f7b336fb1

S&P Global-based reporting said commercial reopening requires recovery in vessel traffic, restored insurance, security improvements, and a 30-45 day observation period after a ceasefire, indicating normalization is unlikely inside the remaining June window. https://www.cnbctv18.com/world/when-is-the-strait-of-hormuz-really-open-again-sp-global-say-5-things-must-happen-first-ws-l-19919615.htm

S&P Global reported broader regional oil and logistics recovery would likely take weeks to months, not just days. https://www.spglobal.com/energy/en/news-research/latest-news/crude-oil/041726-middle-east-oil-recovery-to-require-months-once-strait-of-hormuz-remains-open
A public tracker using IMF PortWatch data showed traffic still at only a small fraction of normal as of June 5, 2026. https://hormuz-traffic.com/

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: IMF PortWatch never publishes a 7-day moving average for Strait of Hormuz 'Arrivals of Ships' equal to or above 60 for any eligible date through June 30, 2026.
supporting_evidence: A PortWatch-linked time series showed the 7-day moving average near 6.00 on May 24, 2026, implying an approximately tenfold increase would be needed to reach 60 before month-end. https://en.macromicro.me/series/30932/imf-hormuz-number-total-ma7
PortWatch continues to classify reduced Hormuz traffic since February 28, 2026 as an ongoing disruption. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/cc317ba850e34c4dadbead6f7b336fb1

PortWatch warns ongoing conflict is causing GPS jamming, AIS spoofing, and vessels going dark in the chokepoint area, consistent with continued severe disruption. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/chokepoint6

S&P Global-based reporting says the Strait cannot be considered commercially reopened without traffic recovery, insurance normalization, security assurance, and time for confidence to return. https://www.cnbctv18.com/world/when-is-the-strait-of-hormuz-really-open-again-sp-global-say-5-things-must-happen-first-ws-l-19919615.htm

S&P Global reported that restoring Middle East oil production and logistics once Hormuz remains open could take from several weeks to many months. https://www.spglobal.com/energy/en/news-research/latest-news/crude-oil/041726-middle-east-oil-recovery-to-require-months-once-strait-of-hormuz-remains-open

Reuters-syndicated reporting described strain on the shipping industry with vessels still stuck inside Hormuz, indicating practical operating constraints remain severe. https://www.msn.com/en-gb/news/world/strain-grows-on-shipping-industry-with-vessels-stuck-inside-hormuz/ar-AA24K4sZ

The WTO Strait of Hormuz Trade Tracker describes major trade flow collapse after the closure, supporting the view that traffic remains far from normal. https://datalab.wto.org/Strait-of-Hormuz-Trade-Tracker
contradicting_evidence: Because the contract resolves YES on any single qualifying date, even a short-lived rebound in reported traffic could still trigger YES if it happens before June 30, 2026. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/faqs

If AIS-related undercounting is materially depressing the published numbers, reported traffic could rise faster than underlying operations alone would suggest once data quality improves. https://portwatch.imf.org/pages/chokepoint6

New evidence indicates a sharp drop in war-risk premiums and a jump in booked Gulf tanker loadings, with brokers reporting more owners returning to the market, which increases the chance of a faster transit recovery before month-end.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 11%, therefore yes_prob = 11%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence materially improves the short-term recovery path because lower war-risk premiums and a large increase in booked tanker loadings are exactly the kind of leading indicators that could accelerate actual transits. That said, the contract still requires the IMF PortWatch 7-day moving average to reach 60 by June 30, and the prior observed level was around 6, so the rebound needed remains very large and the moving-average lag still works against a late-month catch-up. I therefore raise YES meaningfully, but NO remains the dominant outcome.

LONDON, June 6 — Average additional war-risk premiums quoted for Gulf cargoes transiting the Strait of Hormuz fell to 0.35% of hull value this week from 1.7% on May 29, according to broker tallies compiled by Marsh Bendix Marine. question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

New evidence says late-counted vote-by-mail ballots have broken sharply toward Steyer, trimming his gap to second place to about 0.4 percentage points, or roughly 28,000 votes, with a large pool of outstanding ballots still potentially favorable to him.

QUESTION: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

INITIAL FORECAST:
{
  "event_model": {
    "key_actors": [
      "Tom Steyer",
      "Xavier Becerra",
      "Steve Hilton",
      "California voters",
      "California Secretary of State and county election officials",
      "Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks",
      "California Democratic and Republican political networks"
    ],
    "key_mechanisms": [
      "California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election",
      "If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship",
      "If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent",
      "The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align"
    ],
    "latent_variables": [
      "Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering",
      "Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass",
      "How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots",
      "If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup"
    ]
  },
  "hypotheses": [
    {
      "id": "H1",
      "description": "The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.",
      "prior_probability": 0.08,
      "posterior_probability": 0.015,
      "supporting_evidence": [
        "Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer",
        "A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/",
        "California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29",
        "AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/"
      ],
      "contradicting_evidence": [
        "California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor",
        "California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california",
        "CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/",
        "Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/"
      ]
    },
    {
      "id": "H2",
      "description": "The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.",
      "prior_probability": 0.92,
      "posterior_probability": 0.985,
      "supporting_evidence": [
        "Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor",
        "California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california",
        "AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/",
        "CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/",
        "Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29"
      ],
      "contradicting_evidence": [
        "Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/",
        "AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/",
        "If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29"
      ]
    }
  ],
  "hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping": "Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 1.5%, therefore yes_prob = 1.5%.",
  "yes_prob": 0.015,
  "confidence": "high",
  "rationale": "The forecast is dominated by one gating fact: Steyer is currently running third in California's June 2, 2026 top-two primary, and only the top two advance to the November general election. While late counting leaves a small residual chance that he overtakes one of the current leaders, the combination of his current deficit and the fact that he already mounted a record-breaking, self-funded campaign makes a Steyer win very unlikely.",
  "evidence_sources": [
    "https://www.google.com/search?q=Tom+Steyer+California+governor+2026",
    "https://www.bing.com/search?q=Tom+Steyer+California+governor+2026",
    "https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_California_gubernatorial_election",
    "https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026",
    "https://news.google.com/search?q=Tom+Steyer+California+governor+2026",
    "https://www.nytimes.com/",
    "https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/upcoming-elections",
    "https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor",
    "https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/",
    "https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/",
    "https://calmatters.org/politics/2025/11/california-governors-race-tom-steyer/",
    "https://calmatters.org/california-voter-guide-2026/governor/",
    "https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/",
    "https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/",
    "https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/",
    "https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california",
    "https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer",
    "https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29"
  ]
}

---
NEW EVIDENCE (received after your initial research):

SACRAMENTO, June 6 — Xavier Becerra's campaign said on Saturday it would not seek a recount or court challenge if late-certified returns lift Tom Steyer into California's top-two gubernatorial runoff, and urged Democrats to unite against Republican Steve Hilton in November. In a joint statement, the California Democratic Party chair and leaders of two major public-sector unions said they would move staff, voter files and coordinated field resources to Steyer immediately if he advances. The announcement would give Steyer a clearer path to consolidate Democratic support in a state where party unity is often decisive in statewide general elections.
---

Using ONLY the information above — your prior structured forecast plus this new evidence — update your world model and produce a revised forecast.

Rules:
- DO NOT search the web, use any tools, or look up any external information.
  Reason entirely from your existing forecast and the new evidence above.
- Update H1 and H2 posterior_probability to reflect what the new evidence implies.
- Update the supporting_evidence or contradicting_evidence list for the affected hypothesis to include a brief note about the new evidence.
- Update yes_prob (must equal H1 posterior_probability).
- Update rationale to explain concisely what changed and why.
- Keep all other fields (key_actors, key_mechanisms, etc.) unchanged.
- Output ONLY a valid JSON object in the exact same structure as the initial forecast above.  No markdown fences, no commentary before or after.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

New evidence says California opened an expedited audit after a ballot-export formatting error omitted some cross-county vote-center tallies from the statewide unofficial governor dashboard, and officials said corrected uploads during the canvass could alter candidate standings.
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

New evidence says an expedited audit is correcting omitted cross-county vote-center tallies and that tens of thousands of records in two large Southern California counties must be reprocessed, which increases the chance that the current top-two ordering is not stable; Steyer's campaign also claims the affected vote centers were concentrated in metropolitan areas where he invested heavily in same-day outreach.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 5.0%, therefore yes_prob = 5.0%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence weakens the main negative thesis by introducing a specific, nontrivial mechanism for the current primary ranking to change: an acknowledged statewide dashboard error that omitted some vote-center tallies, with tens of thousands of records in large Southern California counties set for reprocessing. That meaningfully raises the chance that Steyer can move from third into the top two during the canvass, especially given the claim that affected areas overlap with his outreach targets. But he still begins from a losing position, still needs to overtake at least one current leader, and still would need to win the general election afterward, so YES rises materially but remains unlikely overall.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

New post-election tabulation evidence indicates about 1.1 million ballots remained, more than 70% from Los Angeles County and Bay Area counties where Becerra was already outperforming Steyer; Steyer reportedly needed to win the outstanding vote by about 9 points statewide to pass Becerra, but late-counted batches had instead favored Becerra by about 6 points, materially worsening Steyer's runoff chances.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

New post-election tabulation evidence indicates the remaining uncounted ballot pool is concentrated in areas where Becerra is already stronger than Steyer, and that recent late-counted batches have favored Becerra rather than Steyer; this sharply lowers the odds of Steyer climbing from third to second.
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.4%, therefore yes_prob = 0.4%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence mostly bears on the key gating step identified in the original forecast: whether Steyer can still reach the top two in the June 2, 2026 primary canvass. It points in the wrong direction for him on both composition and trend. The remaining ballots are concentrated in places where Becerra is already running ahead of Steyer, Steyer reportedly needs an implausibly large margin on the outstanding vote to overtake Becerra, and the late-counted ballots processed so far have moved toward Becerra rather than Steyer. That makes the already-small chance of a runoff comeback materially smaller, so the probability that Steyer ultimately wins the governorship falls further.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

New evidence: on June 6, Steyer said he would not request a recount or file any court challenge and would respect the canvass, while election lawyers said that sharply reduced the chances of any post-election process changing the top-two ordering before certification.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

New evidence: on June 6, Steyer said he would not request a recount or file any court challenge and would respect the canvass, and election lawyers said this sharply reduced the chance that any post-election process would alter the leading order before certification.
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.5%, therefore yes_prob = 0.5%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence weakens the already-small residual path to a Steyer win by removing one of the few mechanisms that could have changed the top-two outcome after election night. In the initial forecast, the remaining YES probability came mostly from uncertainty around late-counted ballots and a possible recount or court process that might reorder a close primary field. Steyer's public decision not to request a recount or pursue litigation, combined with election lawyers' view that this sharply reduces the odds of any post-election change in ordering, makes it even less likely that he can reach the November general election at all. The forecast therefore moves from very unlikely to nearly closed off.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

New evidence indicates the ballot-curing deadline has expired in key counties and that most remaining work is now administrative tabulation of already-accepted ballots rather than restoring rejected ballots, sharply reducing the channels through which Steyer could still expand his total late in the canvass.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

New evidence says the cure deadline for signature mismatches and missing identification has expired and that most remaining county work is administrative tabulation of already-accepted ballots, meaning campaigns now have little room to generate additional valid votes through late voter-contact operations.
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.7%, therefore yes_prob = 0.7%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence makes Steyer's already narrow comeback path even smaller. In the initial forecast, the main residual uncertainty was whether late-counted or cured ballots could still reorder the top two. The update says the deadline to cure signature and ID problems has already expired and that most remaining work is simply tabulating ballots that were already accepted, not adding newly salvaged votes. That does not make a reorder impossible, but it reduces the chance that Steyer can materially expand his total during the canvass, so the forecast shifts further toward NO.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC decision desks
California Democratic and Republican political networks
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
If Steyer does not finish in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary, he cannot win the governorship
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic lean would materially improve his odds against a Republican opponent
The market resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the same winner, or official certification if calls do not align
latent_variables: Whether late-counted ballots materially change the current primary ordering
Whether Steyer can overtake either Becerra or Hilton during the canvass
How representative the current counted vote is of the remaining uncounted ballots
If Steyer were to advance, the strength of his eventual November matchup

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

The new June 6 administrative update says the Secretary of State is only delaying the nightly public posting of county statement-of-vote PDFs during scheduled maintenance, while counties continue canvassing on the normal timetable, which does not create a substantive new path for Steyer to improve his standing.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

The new June 6 update indicates only a temporary delay in when supporting county PDFs appear on the public website, while counties keep canvassing normally, which suggests the change is cosmetic/informational rather than something that improves Steyer's actual electoral position.
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 1.4%, therefore yes_prob = 1.4%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence is mildly negative for Steyer's already-small path because it clarifies that the apparent reporting change is only a temporary delay in public posting of county PDFs, not a change to canvassing or ballot processing. That means there is no new operational reason to expect the underlying vote order to move in his favor; the only remaining path is still a genuine late-count reversal from third into the top two, which remains very unlikely.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer ultimately wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he first qualifies for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two of the June 2, 2026 primary and then is the candidate called the winner by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or is the certified winner.
supporting_evidence: Steyer was a real candidate on the June 2, 2026 primary ballot for California governor, so the scenario is procedurally live. https://ballotpedia.org/Tom_Steyer

A late Emerson/Inside California Politics poll had Becerra at 28%, Steyer at 22%, and Hilton at 21%, showing Steyer had genuine pre-election viability and a plausible runoff path. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

California is a strongly Democratic state in gubernatorial ratings, which would help Steyer if he somehow reached the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

AP noted California's count can shift as late ballots are processed, so the current third-place position is not mathematically final yet. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: California's official results page currently shows Steve Hilton first, Xavier Becerra second, and Tom Steyer third, making Steyer's path dependent on overtaking at least one of them in the canvass. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's top-two primary rules mean only the top two candidates advance to the general election; if Steyer remains third, H1 is impossible. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

CalMatters' election coverage also showed Becerra and Hilton leading the primary while Steyer trailed, reinforcing that the realized vote is worse than his best late polling. https://calmatters.org/politics/elections/2026/06/california-primary-election-results-june-2026/

Steyer spent extraordinary sums on the race, reported by CalMatters as the most expensive gubernatorial campaign in state history, yet still ran third, implying limited upside from additional persuasion. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

The new NBC evidence concerns internal decision-desk management and explicitly says the network's election-calling criteria are unchanged, so it does not create a new substantive path for Steyer to win.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including because he fails to finish in the top two primary, loses the general election, withdraws, or otherwise is not the certified/called winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California returns currently place Steyer third behind Hilton and Becerra, which is the single most important fact because only two candidates advance. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's primary system explicitly advances only the top two candidates to November, so remaining in third would eliminate Steyer before the general election. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

AP's live results page also had Hilton and Becerra ahead of Steyer and did not present Steyer as currently occupying a qualifying position. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

CalMatters reported Steyer's massive self-funding and record-setting spending, making his underperformance more informative and reducing the chance that hidden campaign capacity will rescue him. https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/04/california-governor-race-financials/

Ballotpedia's June-primary overview identified Steyer as a major candidate in a crowded field, but the post-vote environment still left him outside the likely top two. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

The new NBC evidence says the network's West Coast decision-desk reorganization does not alter its election-calling criteria, which reinforces that resolution mechanics are essentially unchanged rather than creating a procedural edge for Steyer.
contradicting_evidence: Steyer did have a credible pre-election path, with late polling placing him in a near tie for second. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
AP cautioned that California counts late ballots for days and weeks, leaving a small chance of a reordered top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

If Steyer somehow advanced, California's Democratic tilt could make him competitive in the general election. https://ballotpedia.org/California_gubernatorial_election%2C_2026_%28June_2_top-two_primary%29

updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The revised forecast is essentially unchanged. That affects operational context around one resolution source but does not change the core gating fact that Steyer is currently in third place in California's top-two primary and likely needs a canvass reversal just to remain alive. question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/

New June 6 evidence says Steyer gained sharply in late-counted vote-by-mail ballots, cutting his gap to second place to 0.4 percentage points, or about 28,000 votes, with roughly 1.1 million ballots still outstanding and that pool reportedly breaking heavily toward him.
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

New June 6 evidence says Steyer has been gaining sharply in late-counted ballots, reducing the gap to second place to just 0.4 percentage points with about 1.1 million ballots outstanding, which materially weakens the view that his elimination is nearly certain.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 18%, therefore yes_prob = 18%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: What changed is that Steyer's biggest obstacle — reaching the top two — now looks materially more surmountable than in the initial forecast. The new evidence says he has been outperforming in late-counted ballots, has cut the deficit for second place to just 0.4 percentage points, and is only about 28,000 votes behind with roughly 1.1 million ballots still outstanding. That does not make him the favorite, because he is still currently third and would still need to win the November general election after any comeback, but it substantially increases the chance that he first reaches the runoff. As a result, the YES probability rises meaningfully from a near-elimination scenario, while NO remains more likely overall.

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/

New evidence indicates that if late-certified returns lift Steyer into the runoff, Becerra would not seek a recount or court challenge and major Democratic Party and union actors would immediately consolidate behind Steyer, improving his conditional general-election path and reducing post-primary procedural friction.
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

New evidence says Becerra would not contest a Steyer advancement and that major Democratic and labor organizations would immediately unify behind Steyer against Hilton, which weakens the NO case by making a Steyer runoff-to-victory sequence more plausible if he slips into second.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 2%, therefore yes_prob = 2%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence modestly improves Steyer's chances because it lowers two important conditional barriers after a possible late-count surge into second place: it reduces the risk of a recount or court fight from Becerra, and it suggests rapid consolidation of Democratic institutional support behind Steyer in a Steyer-vs.-Hilton runoff. That meaningfully strengthens Steyer's hypothetical general-election path. However, the dominant obstacle remains unchanged: official unofficial results still had Steyer in third place after the June 2, 2026 primary, so he still first needs a favorable late-count reversal just to reach November. Because that qualification hurdle remains the main bottleneck, the overall YES probability rises only modestly, from 1% to 2%.
