NEW EVIDENCE (received after your initial research):

SACRAMENTO, June 6 — Xavier Becerra's campaign said on Saturday it would not seek a recount or court challenge if late-certified returns lift Tom Steyer into California's top-two gubernatorial runoff, and urged Democrats to unite against Republican Steve Hilton in November. In a joint statement, the California Democratic Party chair and leaders of two major public-sector unions said they would move staff, voter files and coordinated field resources to Steyer immediately if he advances. The announcement would give Steyer a clearer path to consolidate Democratic support in a state where party unity is often decisive in statewide general elections.
---

Using ONLY the information above — your prior structured forecast plus this new evidence — update your world model and produce a revised forecast.

Rules:
- DO NOT search the web, use any tools, or look up any external information.

- Update H1 and H2 posterior_probability to reflect what the new evidence implies.
- Update the supporting_evidence or contradicting_evidence list for the affected hypothesis to include a brief note about the new evidence.
- Update yes_prob (must equal H1 posterior_probability).
- Update rationale to explain concisely what changed and why.
- Keep all other fields (key_actors, key_mechanisms, etc.) unchanged.
- Output ONLY a valid JSON object in the exact same structure as the initial forecast above.  No markdown fences, no commentary before or after.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/

New evidence says California opened an expedited audit after a ballot-export formatting error omitted some cross-county vote-center tallies from the statewide unofficial governor dashboard, and corrected uploads during the canvass could alter candidate standings.

The new evidence also says two large Southern California counties must reprocess tens of thousands of records and that affected vote centers were concentrated in metropolitan areas where Steyer had invested heavily in same-day voter outreach.
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

Even under the new evidence, officials said the issue did not affect cast ballots themselves, only the statewide unofficial dashboard export, so Steyer still needs the corrected tally to move him into the top two and then still must win the general election.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

New evidence weakens confidence in the unofficial dashboard standings, but it does not establish that the missing records favor Steyer enough to overcome his deficit, nor does it solve his additional need to win the November general election if he reaches it.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

The new evidence says an expedited audit found a ballot-export formatting error that omitted some cross-county vote-center tallies from the statewide unofficial governor dashboard, with tens of thousands of records to be reprocessed in two large Southern California counties, so the apparent third-place standing is less reliable than previously assumed.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 4%, therefore yes_prob = 4%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence materially weakens the key premise behind the initial 1% estimate: Steyer's apparent third-place position on the statewide unofficial dashboard may partly reflect an export-formatting error rather than only valid counted returns. Because corrected files from two large Southern California counties could alter standings, Steyer's runoff path is meaningfully more plausible than before. Still, officials said the issue did not affect cast ballots themselves, only reporting, and Steyer would still need both a favorable correction large enough to reach the top two and then a general-election win. That raises YES from near-impossible to still very unlikely.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

New evidence indicates about 1.1 million ballots remained uncounted, concentrated in Los Angeles County and Bay Area counties where Becerra was already outperforming Steyer; Political Data Inc. estimated Steyer would need to win the outstanding vote by roughly 9 points statewide to pass Becerra for second, but late-counted batches had instead favored Becerra by about 6 points.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

New evidence indicates the remaining ballot pool is concentrated in areas where Becerra is already stronger than Steyer, and recent late-counted ballots have been favoring Becerra rather than narrowing Steyer's deficit, making a runoff comeback materially less likely.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.3%, therefore yes_prob = 0.3%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence makes Steyer's already narrow path even worse. Previously, there was at least a theoretical chance that late-counted Democratic ballots might improve his standing. The update says the remaining uncounted ballots are concentrated in regions where Becerra is already outperforming Steyer, that Steyer would need to win the outstanding vote by roughly 9 points statewide to overtake Becerra for second place, and that the late batches counted so far have actually favored Becerra by about 6 points. That sharply reduces the plausibility of a late-count reversal, and because Steyer would still need to win the general election after that, the overall YES probability falls further.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

New evidence: on June 6, Steyer said he would not request a recount or file any court challenge, and election lawyers said that decision sharply reduced the odds that any post-election process could alter the top-two ordering before certification.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

New evidence: on June 6, Steyer said he would not request a recount or file any court challenge and would respect the canvass, which substantially weakens any remaining procedural path for him to move into the top two before certification.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.3%, therefore yes_prob = 0.3%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: What changed is that Steyer has now publicly ruled out a recount or court challenge and said he will respect the canvass. In the initial forecast, a tiny residual YES path remained because late-count shifts, recounts, or litigation could in principle still alter the top-two ordering before certification. The new evidence materially weakens that residual procedural path. Steyer still appears to need a late-count reversal just to reach November, and by declining to pursue post-election remedies he makes an already unlikely comeback even less plausible. That pushes the forecast from merely very unlikely to almost entirely dependent on an extraordinary canvass-only reversal.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

New evidence indicates ballot-curing deadlines have expired in major counties and most remaining work is simple tabulation of already-accepted ballots, sharply reducing Steyer's ability to gain ground through voter-contact efforts on rejected or provisional ballots.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

New evidence says signature-mismatch and missing-ID cure windows have expired and county officials now describe the canvass as largely administrative, which means there is less room than previously thought for Steyer to improve his standing through restoration of rejected ballots.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 0.5%, therefore yes_prob = 0.5%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence weakens the main remaining pro-Steyer pathway. In the initial forecast, a small residual chance remained that late-counted or cured ballots could materially improve his standing and perhaps push him into the top two. The update says cure deadlines for signature mismatches and missing ID have now expired in major counties, and officials characterize the remaining canvass as mostly tabulation of already-accepted ballots rather than correction of rejected ones. That does not eliminate all uncertainty from the count, but it meaningfully reduces the scope for campaign-driven gains and makes a late reversal from third place even less likely. Because Steyer still would need both a runoff berth and then a general-election win, the overall YES probability falls further.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

New evidence says the Secretary of State's temporary shift of nightly county statement-of-vote PDF postings from 8 p.m. to 10 p.m. is only a public website timing change and that counties will continue canvassing normally, so it does not materially improve Steyer's comeback odds.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

New evidence indicates the temporary delay in posting county statement-of-vote PDFs affects only public document timing, while counties continue canvassing on the normal schedule, so there is no substantive operational development that improves Steyer's electoral position.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 1%, therefore yes_prob = 1%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence does not materially change the forecast. A temporary shift in the nightly posting time of county statement-of-vote PDFs affects public visibility of supporting documents, not the actual canvass, ballot counting, or certification timetable. Because counties continue submitting files and canvassing under the normal schedule, this update adds only minor process noise and does not meaningfully improve Steyer's already narrow path from third place into the top two and then to a statewide general-election win.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

The new NBC memo about appointing Marisa Chen to oversee West Coast decision-desk operations explicitly says the reorganization does not change NBC's election-calling criteria, so it does not create a new plausible path for Steyer through altered network resolution standards.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

The new NBC memo indicates only a staffing/organizational change in decision-desk operations and explicitly says NBC's criteria for calling elections are unchanged, which leaves the substantive case against a Steyer win essentially intact.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 1%, therefore yes_prob = 1%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence is largely resolution-process noise rather than outcome-relevant evidence. NBC's appointment of a new senior director for West Coast decision-desk operations slightly updates the world model around who may supervise one of the market's call sources, but the memo explicitly says NBC's election-calling criteria are unchanged. Because the market also falls back to official certification if network calls do not align, this staffing change does not materially improve Steyer's already remote path. He still appears to need a late-count primary comeback just to reach November and then a general-election win, so the forecast remains essentially unchanged.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California voters in the top-two primary and general election
California Secretary of State and county election officials
Associated Press, Fox News, and NBC as market resolution call sources
key_mechanisms: Steyer must first qualify for the November 3, 2026 general election by finishing in the top two in California's June 2, 2026 primary
Late-counted vote-by-mail and provisional ballots can shift unofficial primary standings before certification
If Steyer reaches the general election, he must then win statewide in November
The market resolves YES only if Steyer is the winner recognized by AP, Fox News, and NBC, or by official certification if network calls do not align
latent_variables: How many uncounted ballots remain and where they are concentrated
Whether late ballots lean Democratic enough to help Steyer overtake Steve Hilton for second place
Whether late Democratic ballots benefit Steyer specifically rather than Xavier Becerra or other Democrats
Steyer's hypothetical general-election strength if he somehow reaches the runoff
Potential recount, certification, or call-timing complications

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer wins the 2026 California gubernatorial election, meaning he ultimately qualifies for the November 2026 general election, wins that election, and is recognized as the winner under the market's resolution rules.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual certified candidate and launched a real campaign for governor: https://www.tomsteyer.com/press/tom-steyer-launches-run-for-california-governor-pledging-to-tackle-affordability-crisis

Pre-election polling showed a plausible path to the top two, with Emerson finding Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%: https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

CEPP polling also placed Becerra, Hilton, and Steyer in the top cluster before the primary: https://www.cpp.edu/class/political-science/docs/media/cepp_2026_california_primary_poll_results_june_1_2026.pdf

Reporting indicated Democrats were returning ballots later than Republicans, raising the possibility that late counts could help Democratic candidates such as Steyer: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/politics/in-californias-packed-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom-democrats-are-shrugging-at-their-choices
Some commentary explicitly argued Steyer could still make the runoff if late ballots broke favorably: https://prospect.org/2026/06/03/good-election-night-for-progressives-steyer-becerra-california/
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State unofficial primary results show Steyer in third place behind Steve Hilton and Xavier Becerra, making runoff qualification unlikely: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The California primary uses a top-two system, so finishing third would eliminate Steyer before the general election: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

NBC Los Angeles live coverage after the primary continued to describe Becerra and Hilton as leading the race, not Steyer: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-2026-primary-election-governor-live-updates/3899066/

Daily News reported Steyer had only about 19.6% of counted votes and merely argued he still had a path, implying he was behind rather than favored: https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
SFGATE described Steyer as trailing in third on election night and still trying to stay optimistic: https://www.sfgate.com/politics/article/tom-steyer-california-governor-22288922.php

Steyer appears to have already spent extraordinary sums yet still trailed, reducing the chance that additional campaigning can reverse the outcome: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/who-is-tom-steyer-california-governor-race-election/3883007/

Additional reporting said Steyer spent roughly $216 million of his own money and still looked on track to be eliminated: https://www.msn.com/en-us/news/politics/tom-steyer-spent-216-million-of-his-own-money-to-run-for-governor-of-california-here-s-where-that-ranks/ar-AA24KMzq
Yahoo reporting similarly framed Steyer as lagging badly despite massive spending: https://www.yahoo.com/news/politics/articles/215-million-spent-lagging-tom-040700861.html

The new Sacramento booking evidence is explicitly described as routine election-cycle planning and not indicative of which candidates are likely to advance or win, so it provides no meaningful support for a Steyer comeback.

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, whether because he fails to qualify for the general election, loses in November, or another candidate is certified/called the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official statewide unofficial results currently place Steyer third, outside the top two needed to advance: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

California's official election results portal confirms that only canvass remains before certification, so the remaining path is a late-count comeback rather than normal campaigning: https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/

AP's California primary results tracker covers the same all-party primary and reflects the race structure in which only the top two advance: https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/

NBC reporting before and after Election Day consistently showed Becerra leading and Steyer fighting Hilton for second rather than occupying a dominant position: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/california-governor-poll-xavier-becerra-steve-hilton-tom-steyer/3897267/

NBC's race coverage on advancement to the November runoff focused on who was moving on, underscoring that Steyer's main problem is making the runoff at all: https://www.nbclosangeles.com/news/local/primary-election-results-november-runoff/3899092/

CalMatters described a crowded field and multiple serious contenders, which lowered the baseline chance of any specific non-incumbent candidate winning: https://calmatters.org/politics/2026/03/california-governor-candidates/

US News/AP noted Republicans have not won a California gubernatorial race since 2010, implying that if Becerra reaches the runoff against Hilton, Steyer is blocked entirely and the eventual winner is likely someone else: https://www.usnews.com/news/politics/articles/2026-06-02/california-holds-crowded-primary-in-race-to-replace-gov-gavin-newsom

The new Sacramento evidence says increased hotel, catering, security, and media bookings are routine fall election planning and explicitly do not signal which candidates are likely to advance or win, so it does not improve Steyer's odds.
contradicting_evidence: California counts ballots slowly and AP notes substantial post-election updates are normal, so current standings are not yet final: https://apnews.com/article/california-primary-ballot-counting-votes-trump-51e814c6a490766276f9a0cc856dc65f
Los Angeles Times reporting suggested uncounted votes could help Democrats in close California races: https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2026-06-03/la-me-late-vote-ca

USA Today framed the race as still close enough that Steyer supporters believed late Democratic ballots could cut into Hilton's lead: https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/elections/2026/06/04/california-governor-steve-hilton-xavier-beccera-tom-steyer/90393157007/
CalMatters reported Democrats were slower to return ballots, which could in principle improve Steyer's position as counting continues: https://calmatters.org/newsletter/democrats-slower-to-vote/

ABC7 coverage highlighted polling that showed Steyer narrowly ahead of Hilton in some late surveys, indicating there was a genuine but narrow runoff path before votes were counted: https://abc7news.com/post/california-governors-race-new-poll-shows-tom-steyer-edging-hilton-battle-2nd-tightens/19207787/

Politico's pre-election analysis treated the race as unsettled and explored multiple paths to victory, showing uncertainty remained before votes were cast: https://www.politico.com/news/magazine/2026/05/31/california-governor-polls-elections-roundtable-00933722

updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence is essentially neutral. Routine post-primary bookings by campaigns, advocacy groups, and media in Sacramento indicate ordinary election-cycle activity heading toward November, but the evidence explicitly says these reservations do not reflect any judgment about which candidates are likely to advance or win. Because it is non-candidate-specific and does not bear on Steyer's main obstacle—currently sitting third in the top-two primary count—the forecast remains effectively unchanged.

question: Will Tom Steyer win the California Governor Election in 2026?

key_actors: California Secretary of State and county election officials
key_mechanisms: California's top-two primary determines which two candidates advance to the November 3, 2026 general election
Ballots postmarked by June 2, 2026 can arrive through June 9, 2026, so late counting can still shift primary standings
If Steyer fails to finish top two in the primary, he cannot win the general election and the market resolves NO
If Steyer reaches the general election, California's Democratic partisan lean would materially improve his chances against a Republican opponent
Polymarket resolves based on AP, Fox News, and NBC calling the same winner, or official certification if the networks do not converge
latent_variables: Whether late-counted and outstanding ballots disproportionately favor Steyer relative to Becerra and Hilton
How much remaining vote is left to count and where it is concentrated geographically
Whether Steyer can overtake Becerra for second place despite a substantial current deficit
Conditional on making the runoff, whether Steyer would face Hilton or Becerra and how that matchup would alter his general-election odds
Whether any recount, tabulation irregularity, or prolonged delay changes the final certified top-two result

hypotheses.description: The market resolves YES: Tom Steyer is ultimately recognized as the winner of the November 3, 2026 California gubernatorial election by AP, Fox News, and NBC all calling the race for him, or by official certification showing he won.
supporting_evidence: Steyer is an actual declared candidate and his campaign remains active, indicating a live candidacy rather than a hypothetical one. https://www.tomsteyer.com/

Late-May polling showed Steyer plausibly competitive for a top-two slot; Emerson had Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/

Berkeley IGS polling also had Steyer in the top tier, though trailing Becerra and near Hilton, implying a nonzero path before votes were counted. https://igs.berkeley.edu/research/berkeley-igs-poll/2026-berkeley-igs-poll-releases

California continues counting ballots after Election Day, with ballots accepted through June 9 if postmarked by June 2 and certification not until July 10, leaving some residual room for a comeback. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/upcoming-elections/primary-election-june-2-2026

New June 6 evidence says late-counted vote-by-mail ballots have broken sharply toward Steyer, cutting his statewide deficit for second place to about 0.4 percentage points, or roughly 28,000 votes, with a large outstanding ballot pool still being processed.
contradicting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State results on June 5 show Steyer in third place at 20.2%, behind Steve Hilton at 27.2% and Xavier Becerra at 26.0%. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

NBC's live results estimate roughly 59.6% of expected vote is in, so Steyer's deficits are large relative to the remaining vote pool. https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/2026-primary-elections/california-governor-results

CBS Los Angeles reported that analyst Paul Mitchell saw late-count patterns that were not especially favorable to Steyer and noted Hilton was doing pretty well among late voters. https://www.cbsnews.com/losangeles/news/california-governors-race-candidates-2026-primary/

California's top-two rules mean Steyer must first overtake at least one of the current top two; otherwise he cannot appear on the general-election ballot for governor. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

Multiple contemporaneous reports described Hilton and Becerra as leading the race while Steyer faced elimination or an increasingly narrow path. https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
KTLA likewise reported Steyer in third place with 20%, behind Hilton and Becerra. https://ktla.com/news/politics/california-governor-race-hilton-becerra/

hypotheses.description: The market resolves NO: Tom Steyer does not win the 2026 California gubernatorial election, including if he fails to finish top two in the primary, loses in the general election, withdraws, or another candidate is certified as the winner.
supporting_evidence: Official California Secretary of State returns show Steyer currently third, a position that would eliminate him under the top-two system. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/returns/governor

The Secretary of State explains that only the top two primary finishers advance to the general election, making primary position the key gating mechanism. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/primary-elections-california

NBC's live results indicate a substantial share of the vote is already counted, making a large reversal less likely. https://www.nbcnews.com/politics/2026-primary-elections/california-governor-results
CBS Los Angeles reported expert analysis that the math and late-vote pattern are not on Steyer's side. https://www.cbsnews.com/losangeles/news/california-governors-race-candidates-2026-primary/

The Daily News framed the question as whether Steyer still had any path to the runoff at all, underscoring how weak his current position is. https://www.dailynews.com/2026/06/04/california-governors-race-does-tom-steyer-still-have-a-path-to-the-november-runoff/
AP's live California governor primary results track the same contest in which Steyer trails the apparent top two. https://apnews.com/projects/elections-2026/california-primary-results-governor/
contradicting_evidence: Pre-primary polling suggested Steyer had a plausible top-two route and was not an irrelevant candidate. https://emersoncollegepolling.com/california-2026-poll-becerra-leads-steyer-and-hilton-toss-up-for-second-spot/
Berkeley IGS also showed Steyer within striking distance of second before the primary. https://igs.berkeley.edu/research/berkeley-igs-poll/2026-berkeley-igs-poll-releases

California's slow ballot-count process means the current unofficial order is not yet final and certification is still weeks away. https://www.sos.ca.gov/elections/upcoming-elections/primary-election-june-2-2026
The official unprocessed-ballot report confirms that outstanding ballots remain to be processed during the canvass period. https://dp.electionresults.sos.ca.gov/unprocessed-ballots-status

New June 6 evidence says Steyer has been gaining sharply in late-counted vote-by-mail ballots, reducing his gap to second to about 0.4 points with roughly 1.1 million outstanding ballots estimated, which materially weakens the view that his runoff path is near-closed.

updated_structured_forecast.hypothesis_to_forecast_mapping: Since H1=YES and H2=NO, yes_prob = posterior_probability(H1) directly. H1 posterior: 15%, therefore yes_prob = 15%.
updated_structured_forecast.rationale: The new evidence materially reopens Steyer's path by showing that late-counted ballots are breaking much more in his favor than the initial model assumed. Instead of needing an almost fantastical comeback, he is now only about 0.4 percentage points, roughly 28,000 votes, behind Becerra for second while a large pool of outstanding ballots remains. That does not make him likely to advance, and he still must first clear the top-two gate before he can benefit from California's Democratic lean in a Hilton matchup, but it raises his overall win probability from a near-elimination tail risk to a still-clear underdog with a real path.
